← All posts February 12, 2026

An infinite product that can't help but vanish

  • math
  • analysis
  • problem-solving

Week three’s problem was an analysis one:

For x∈Rx \in \mathbb{R}x∈R, show that ∏i=1∞sin⁡ ⁣(xi)=0.\prod_{i=1}^{\infty} \sin\!\left(\frac{x}{i}\right) = 0.∏i=1∞​sin(ix​)=0.

The intuition is quick: as i→∞i \to \inftyi→∞, the arguments x/ix/ix/i shrink to 0, so the factors sin⁡(x/i)\sin(x/i)sin(x/i) shrink to 0 too. You’re multiplying by numbers arbitrarily close to zero, forever. The work is making that airtight, since “the terms go to 0” is not by itself a theorem about products.

Step 1: the factors tend to zero. For any ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0, the Archimedean property gives an N>∣x∣/ϵN > |x|/\epsilonN>∣x∣/ϵ, so ∣x/i∣<ϵ|x/i| < \epsilon∣x/i∣<ϵ for all i≥Ni \ge Ni≥N. Hence x/i→0x/i \to 0x/i→0, and by continuity of sine,

lim⁡i→∞sin⁡ ⁣(xi)=sin⁡(0)=0.\lim_{i \to \infty} \sin\!\left(\frac{x}{i}\right) = \sin(0) = 0.limi→∞​sin(ix​)=sin(0)=0.

(If x=0x = 0x=0 every factor is already 0 and there’s nothing to prove.)

Step 2: split the product. Because the factors tend to 0, there’s an MMM such that

∣sin⁡ ⁣(xi)∣≤12for all i>M.\left|\sin\!\left(\frac{x}{i}\right)\right| \le \frac{1}{2} \quad \text{for all } i > M.​sin(ix​)​≤21​for all i>M.

Write the partial product Pn=∏i=1nsin⁡(x/i)P_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \sin(x/i)Pn​=∏i=1n​sin(x/i) as a fixed head times a tail:

∣Pn∣=∣∏i=1Msin⁡ ⁣(xi)∣⏟= C, a constant⋅∣∏i=M+1nsin⁡ ⁣(xi)∣≤C⋅(12)n−M.|P_n| = \underbrace{\left|\prod_{i=1}^{M} \sin\!\left(\frac{x}{i}\right)\right|}_{=\,C, \text{ a constant}} \cdot \left|\prod_{i=M+1}^{n} \sin\!\left(\frac{x}{i}\right)\right| \le C \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n-M}.∣Pn​∣==C, a constant​i=1∏M​sin(ix​)​​​⋅​∏i=M+1n​sin(ix​)​≤C⋅(21​)n−M.

Step 3: squeeze. The geometric bound dies:

0≤lim⁡n→∞∣Pn∣≤lim⁡n→∞C(12)n−M=0,0 \le \lim_{n \to \infty} |P_n| \le \lim_{n \to \infty} C \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n-M} = 0,0≤limn→∞​∣Pn​∣≤limn→∞​C(21​)n−M=0,

so Pn→0P_n \to 0Pn​→0 and the infinite product is 0. ■\blacksquare■

The pattern — quarantine a finite, badly-behaved prefix into a constant, then beat the infinite tail with a geometric bound — shows up constantly in analysis. The head can be as ugly as it wants; only the tail’s behavior decides the limit.
